1:
The chapter opens with a diplomatic overture, and the witnesses read it first as history and then as providence. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the man and the moment. Ellicott traces Hiram back to 2 Samuel 5:11 and its parallel 1 Chronicles 14:1, where he had already furnished David with workmen and materials and is styled 'a lover of David'; from Dios and Menander, whom Josephus cites out of the Tyrian archives, he gathers a prosperous, building-minded, and probably tributary monarch, so that this first embassy is one of congratulation and perhaps of renewed fealty. Keil and Delitzsch set the verse in its frame: no sooner was his kingdom consolidated than Solomon began to prepare for the temple, first by treating with Hiram for cedar, cypress, hewn stone, and a skilled artist (vv.1–12), then by raising a levy from his own people (vv.13–18) — the whole running parallel to 2 Chronicles 2, which they judge a free and independent working of a common source. Gill adds the philological curiosities, that the Phoenician writers call this king Hirom, Eupolemus names him Suron, and the Chronicler Huram.

The Puritan Matthew Henry hears the affection behind the diplomacy. Tyre was a trading city on the sea-border of Israel, never among the devoted nations nor at enmity with them, so that David had always lived at peace with her; and Hiram, 'ever a lover of David,' Henry reckons a worshipper of the true God who had renounced, though he could not reform, the idolatry of his city — for a godly man's character will win the affections even of those who are without. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke settles the one disputed point of fact: because this Hiram both congratulated Solomon and had earlier built David's palace (2 Samuel 5:11), some have supposed two kings of the name, but Clarke finds 'no very solid reason' for it — the same Hiram loved the son because he had always loved the father.

The single real division, then, is Clarke's question whether one Hiram or two stand behind these chapters, and the witnesses incline together to one. Beneath it they are agreed on the providence. Gill's condoling-and-congratulating embassy, Henry's old friendship, and the grammatical-historical dating all converge to show a heathen king drawn, at the very outset of the reign, into the service of God's house — the first quiet hint of the theme this chapter will unfold, that the temple of Israel's God would rise in no small part by Gentile hands.
2:
The reply of Solomon draws the witnesses to two things: the meaning of building 'to the name of the Lord,' and the authenticity of the correspondence itself. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the embassy as Solomon's formal announcement of the design his father had projected, together with his request for building-wood from Lebanon. The words 'Thou knowest that my father David could not build' show, they argue, that David had long been busy with the plan and had already opened negotiations with Hiram, which agrees with 1 Chronicles 22:4. The phrase 'to the name of Jehovah' they ground in Deuteronomy 12:5 and Deuteronomy 12:11, the place the Lord would choose 'to put His name there'; and the Name, they insist, is no mere reputation or numen known and celebrated by men, but the manifestation of the divine nature in a visible sign, a real pledge of God's presence — which is why 2 Samuel 7:13, to which Solomon appeals, speaks interchangeably of a house 'for me' and a house 'for my name.'

The reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Clarke turn instead to the letters that passed between the two kings. Gill reports that Josephus appealed to the very archives of Tyre for the genuineness of this correspondence, and that Eupolemus, a heathen writer, preserved both Solomon's letter and Hiram's answer in terms that agree with the sacred record. Clarke grants the interchange of ambassadors and friendly greetings, and notes the same claim that the letters were kept in the Tyrian archives down to Josephus's day — but adds his characteristic caution that this, 'like many other assertions of the same author, is worthy of little credit.'

So the one division here is not doctrinal but historical: whether the archived letters Josephus cites are a genuine deposit (Gill, with the heathen witness of Eupolemus) or a later embellishment (Clarke's suspicion of Josephus). Beneath it the witnesses agree on the substance of the verse — that Solomon builds not to his own glory but 'to the name of the Lord his God,' and that the house is to be, as Keil and Delitzsch put it, the appointed sign and pledge of the presence of the God who had promised it to his father.
3:
Solomon's letter turns on a reason and a contrast: David could not build 'for the wars which were about him on every side.' The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history. Keil and Delitzsch read the clause of David's constant warfare 'until the Lord put them under the soles of his feet,' the rest he never enjoyed as a lasting possession (compare 2 Samuel 7:1); and, looking ahead, they carefully guard the statement that there was now 'no adversary' as not at variance with the later rising of Hadad in 1 Kings 11:14, which belonged to a much later time. Ellicott adds the corroborating detail that 1 Chronicles 22:4 records how 'the Zidonians and they of Tyre brought much cedar wood to David,' so that Hiram already knew well the old king's desire to build, and the care with which, being disappointed of it himself, he had laid up materials for the happier experience of his successor.

The reformed Gill fills in how Hiram came to know all this: partly by common fame, and partly from David himself, between whom and Hiram there had been dealings in the very matter of cedars for building. The verse thus looks two ways at once — back to the seasoned warrior whose sword had won the peace, and forward to the son who would enjoy it.

Where the witnesses land together is on the theology of the delay. It was no defect in David's zeal that kept him from the work, but the providence that fills a reign of war with the winning of rest and reserves the building of the house of peace for the reign that follows. David gathered the cedar; Solomon would raise the walls. The interpreters do not quarrel here; they agree that God apportions to each servant his own part in a single work, and that the very wars which forbade the father to build were the wars that secured the peace in which the son could. The clause 'until the Lord put them under the soles of his feet' the commentators do not pass over lightly, for it names a hard-won victory and not a natural calm; the rest Solomon inherits is itself the fruit of the Lord's own subduing of David's enemies, so that, on every reading, the house of God would be built on ground the Lord had first cleared.
4:
'The Lord my God hath given me rest on every side.' The witnesses hear in the verse both a fact of Solomon's reign and a parable of the church's work. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the rest as one 'such as David never enjoyed for a permanency' (compare 2 Samuel 7:1), and read 'no evil occurrent' of just such disasters as had marred the father's reign — the rebellions of Absalom and Sheba, the pestilence at the numbering of the people; while 'no adversary is there' they again distinguish from Hadad's later enterprise in 1 Kings 11:14. The Wesleyan Clarke fastens on the Hebrew: eyn satan, 'there is no satan,' no opposer nor any kind of evil, all peace and quiet within and without — God has given this quiet that the temple may be built; Deus nobis haec otia fecit.

That word satan opens the Puritan Matthew Henry's richest note. There is now no adversary, 'no instrument of Satan to oppose it, or to divert us from it'; and Henry generalizes the lesson: Satan does all he can to hinder temple work (1 Thessalonians 2:18; Zechariah 3:1), but when he is bound (Revelation 20:2) we should be busy. When there is no evil occurrent, then let us be vigorous and zealous in what is good and get it forward; when the churches have rest, let them be edified (Acts 9:31). Days of peace and prosperity present a fair gale, for which we must answer if we do not improve it.

The reformed Gill states the plain sense — rest from foreign enemies, for Solomon had wars with none — and the witnesses close in one mind. The peace is God's gift, and it is God's opportunity. The quiet that David's sword had purchased and that God now bestowed was not given for ease but for building; and the same holds, the interpreters agree, for every season of rest the church is granted, which is lent us not to idle in but to build in. Clarke's Latin tag — that God himself 'made us this ease' — and Henry's fair gale of prosperity meet in one conviction, that the tranquillity of the reign is neither accident nor mere policy but a gift bestowed with a purpose; and the witnesses press it home, that the peace which hushes every adversary is granted less for repose than for the raising of God's house.
5:
'Behold, I purpose to build an house unto the name of the Lord my God.' The witnesses take the verse as the meeting-point of Solomon's resolve and God's promise. The reformed Gill reads the purpose simply and reverently — a house for God's worship, and for his honour and glory. The Wesleyan Clarke presses the phrase 'unto the name of the Lord' into a small theology: 'The name of God is God himself,' so that Solomon purposes to build a house to that infinite and eternal Being called Jehovah, not to a mere title.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch anchor the resolve in the covenant. The clause 'as the Lord spake unto David my father' points, they show, to 2 Samuel 7:12 and 2 Samuel 7:13, the promise that the son whom God would set upon the throne should build a house to his name; the very grammar of the announcement (they note the idiom of 'said' followed by an infinitive) marks it as a settled intention rather than a hope. Solomon does not invent the project; he executes a word already given.

The Puritan Henry draws out the encouragement in that word. God had told David that his son should build him a house (2 Samuel 7:5); Solomon, therefore, 'will take it as a pleasure to be thus employed, and will not lose the honour designed him by that promise.' Here Henry finds a general spur: it may stir us much to good undertakings to be assured of good success in them — 'let God's promise quicken our endeavours.' The witnesses do not divide. Gill's worship, Clarke's Name, Keil and Delitzsch's covenant, and Henry's encouragement all rest on the one foundation, that the temple is not the fruit of royal ambition but the keeping of a divine word, undertaken in the confidence that what God has promised he will also prosper. So the resolve of the verse is at once personal and covenantal: Solomon names the house 'unto the name of the Lord my God,' claiming the promise as his own inheritance while owning it as a trust; and Gill's threefold end — worship, honour, and glory — keeps before us the purpose for which the whole enterprise is undertaken, that a work begun upon a divine word may be carried on in the sure hope of a divine blessing.
6:
Solomon's request for cedars gathers the fullest comment in the chapter, and the witnesses read it for its geography, its craftsmanship, and its lesson on gifts. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that Solomon asked not for cedar only but (as 1 Kings 5:8 shows) for cypress too, and, by the fuller account of 2 Chronicles 2:6, for a skilled artist — a request passed over here but made good later when Hiram sent the craftsman of 1 Kings 7:13; and they locate the celebrated cedar forest two days north of Beirut, on that part of Lebanon which belonged to Hiram's kingdom. Ellicott dwells on the timber itself, so precious to comparatively treeless Palestine: the forest was proverbial for beauty and fragrance (Song of Solomon 4:11; Hosea 14:6), watered by streams from the snowy heights (Jeremiah 18:14) when the lowlands were parched, its cedars 'the Lord had planted' (Psalms 104:16); Solomon's request, he notes, is 'couched almost in the language of command,' and asks chiefly for skilled labour in felling and working the wood, the 'fir' of Hiram's answer being almost certainly the cypress (2 Chronicles 2:8).

The Wesleyan Clarke turns to the craft. 'Skill to hew timber' he calls an obsolete phrase for knowing how to cut it, and insists it is no small art: from Vitruvius he rehearses the rules — fell in autumn or winter and in the wane of the moon, cut gradually toward the pith to let the sap drain, season the wood for three years before using it — and ends with a rueful aside at his own age: 'We do everything in a hurry, and our building is good for nothing.' In this skill the Sidonians, Hiram's subjects, excelled all Israel.

That excellence gives the Puritan Henry his theme. The trees were Solomon's, but the Israelites had not the Sidonians' skill to hew them; Canaan was a land of wheat and barley (Deuteronomy 8:8), so that its people were versed in husbandry, not manufacture. Yet Henry weighs the two gifts and knows which is greater: 'True piety is a much more valuable gift of heaven than the highest degree of ingenuity — better be an Israelite skilful in the law than a Sidonian skilful to hew timber.' He hears the whole scene echoed in the prophet's vision of the sons of strangers building the wall of the gospel temple (Isaiah 60:10) and the glory of Lebanon brought to beautify it (Isaiah 60:13). The witnesses agree without strife: God so divides his gifts that one people needs another, and the highest endowment is not the artisan's cunning but the piety that builds to God's name.
7:
Hiram's response — 'Blessed be the Lord this day, which hath given unto David a wise son' — raises the chapter's sharpest question: how far does this pagan king truly worship the God he blesses? The grammatical-historical commentators are the most guarded. Keil and Delitzsch record simply that Hiram 'rejoiced greatly' and returned his consent in writing (2 Chronicles 2:11). Ellicott, weighing the fuller and still more deferential language of 2 Chronicles 2:12, judges that Hiram's acknowledgment of Jehovah, the God of Israel, is 'a token rather of such deference to Israel than of any acceptance of Him as the one true God' — a diplomat's courtesy, not a convert's creed.

The Wesleyan Clarke and the Puritan Henry read him more warmly. Clarke thinks the blessing shows Hiram 'a worshipper of the true God,' unless (as with many heathen) he supposed that every country had its own deity and merely thanked the God of Israel for giving so wise a prince to his friends — and 'the first opinion seems to be the most correct.' Henry dwells not on Hiram's theology but on his generosity: he rejoiced that Solomon trod in his father's steps, and gave God the praise for it; from which Henry draws the lesson not to envy others their gifts, and the comfort of seeing religion and wisdom kept up in families from one generation to another, a godly seed being 'a token for good' (Malachi 2:15). The reformed Gill simply marks the occasion — Hiram spoke thus when Solomon's letter was read to him.

So the witnesses divide, gently, over the man's heart: a real, if imperfect, worshipper of the true God (Clarke, with Henry warm toward him) or a courteous pagan whose blessing is deference to Israel (Ellicott). What none of them disputes is the striking thing the verse itself records — that a Gentile king is made to bless the Lord, and to celebrate as God's gift the very wisdom that v.12 will name; so that even Tyre is drawn in to praise the God whose house its cedars would help to build.
8:
'And Hiram sent to Solomon' — a bare line of narrative that the witnesses read as a small study in wisdom's prudence. The reformed Gill notes only that it introduces a letter, 'to the following purpose'; but the Puritan Henry makes the verse a lesson in careful dealing. Hiram, he observes, 'deliberated upon the proposal before he returned an answer,' as the very words of the verse imply — 'I have considered the things.' It is common, Henry warns, for those who make bargains rashly afterward to wish them unmade again; as the virtuous woman 'considereth a field, and buyeth it' (Proverbs 31:16), so those do not lose time who take time to consider. And Hiram descended to particulars, settling each point so that no misunderstanding should later breed a quarrel.

The chief particular was the carriage of the timber, on which the verse's own arrangement turns. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, reading the reply together with v.9, render Hiram's undertaking to bring the trees down from Lebanon to the sea, bind them into rafts, and float them to the place Solomon should appoint, where they would be taken apart and received — the landing-place being Joppa, the nearest harbour to Jerusalem (2 Chronicles 2:16; compare Joshua 19:46). Land-carriage over such a distance would have been ruinous; the sea-route was the counsel of prudence.

Brief as it is, the verse thus does real work in the story. It marks the moment when goodwill becomes a settled agreement, each party knowing exactly what he undertakes. The witnesses do not differ; together they hear in Hiram's considered answer the same wisdom the chapter keeps praising — the wisdom that thinks before it promises, defines before it disputes, and by plain dealing keeps a friendship whole. What Solomon must yet supply, the payment in food for Hiram's household, the next verses will settle in the same careful spirit. Gill's single line, that the verse opens a letter, and Keil and Delitzsch's care over the rafts and the harbour meet in Henry's larger lesson — that wisdom shows itself as much in the manner of agreeing as in the matter agreed. The considered answer, the settled particulars, the route chosen to spare a ruinous overland haul: each is a mark of the prudence the chapter keeps ascribing, in the end, to the gift of God.
9:
Hiram's plan for moving the timber, and his price of 'food for my household,' draw the witnesses to the logistics of the sea and the providence of trade. The grammatical-historical commentators map the route. Keil and Delitzsch render the undertaking exactly: the servants bring the trees down from Lebanon to the sea, Hiram binds them into rafts and floats them to the appointed place, has them taken apart there, and Solomon fetches them. Ellicott adds that the timber was let down on slides along the face of the mountain toward the shore and then rafted round to Joppa (2 Chronicles 2:16), sparing the enormous cost of land-carriage; and he seizes on the striking exchange of 'food for his household' instead of money, for it explains what is recorded ages later in Acts 12:20 — that the country of the Tyrians was 'nourished' by Palestine. Their narrow coastal strip, backed by Lebanon and given over to seamanship rather than tillage, could never feed its crowded population; and in the great catalogue of Tyre's imports, Judah and Israel appear as supplying 'wheat, and honey, and oil, and balm' (Ezekiel 27:17).

The Wesleyan Clarke fills in the seafaring detail: the timber, squared and cut to scantling at Lebanon, could be floated down the coast and landed at Joppa or Jamnia, about twenty-five miles from Jerusalem, so neatly prepared that it needed no further axe or saw when it arrived (1 Kings 6:7). The reformed Gill simply identifies 'the sea' as the Mediterranean, on which Tyre stood.

Under the practical arrangements the witnesses hear one settled truth, which the Puritan Henry states best: 'by the wise disposal of Providence, one country has need of another, and is benefited by another, that there may be mutual correspondence and dependence, to the glory of God our common parent.' Tyre had the craft and the ships; Israel had the corn and the oil. The temple would rise as the two supplied each other's lack — a small picture, the interpreters agree, of the God who so orders the nations that none is sufficient to itself and all are bound together in his service.
10:
With Hiram's delivery of the timber the bargain is performed on his side, and the witnesses read the verse for the beauty of a covenant kept. The Puritan Matthew Henry leads: each party 'made good his engagement.' Hiram delivered Solomon the cedar and fir according to his word; and though the trees were Solomon's own, Henry notes with the old tag Materiam superabat opus — the workmanship was of more value than the material — that Hiram is rightly said to 'give' the trees, since his servants' skilled labour in felling and preparing them was the greater part of the gift. Solomon in turn would convey the corn he had promised (v.11); and Henry presses the moral: 'thus let justice be followed' (Deuteronomy 16:20), justice on both sides, in every bargain.

The reformed Gill adds the plain mechanics — Hiram ordered his servants to cut the trees from Lebanon and sent them floating in rafts, which Solomon received — and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch round off the transaction with the text's own summary, that 'Hiram gave Solomon cedar trees and fir trees according to all his desire.' The whole of Solomon's request was met; nothing was withheld.

The interpreters do not divide. What they draw from the verse together is the honesty that makes an alliance lasting: two kings, unequal in wealth and power, each faithfully rendering what he owed the other. Henry's insistence on 'justice on both sides' is not incidental but central, for it is this fair dealing, crowned by the wisdom God gave Solomon, that will 'ripen their mutual love into a mutual league' in the verses that follow. The timber that would frame the house of God came, the witnesses observe, not by extortion but by an honest exchange kept scrupulously on both sides. Henry's repeated word, 'justice on both sides,' carries the weight of his whole note: a bargain is not honourable unless both parties are, and the greatness of the work is no excuse for the smallness of a broken promise. Gill's picture of the rafts received and Keil and Delitzsch's 'according to all his desire' show the compact kept to the letter on Hiram's part, as the following verses will show it kept on Solomon's. It is a quiet commendation of plain dealing in the service of God — the very materials of the sanctuary gathered by an honest exchange, so that no injustice was built into its walls.
11:
Solomon's payment — twenty thousand measures of wheat and (in the Hebrew) twenty measures of oil, year by year — sets the witnesses to reckoning quantities and weighing readings. The reformed Gill labours the arithmetic: the measure is the Hebrew cor, which he reckons at some 17,477 cubic inches, equal to ten ephahs; so that twenty thousand of them amount to an immense provision, by his calculation nearly thirteen million pounds of wheat. The figure alone shows the scale of the traffic between the two kingdoms.

The Wesleyan Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott, however, find the account here 'deficient' and correct it from the fuller parallel of 2 Chronicles 2:10, which stipulates twenty thousand measures of beaten wheat, twenty thousand of barley, twenty thousand baths of wine, and twenty thousand baths of oil. Clarke suggests the wheat here named may be for Hiram's own household and the fuller list for his workmen, and notes that for the Hebrew's 'twenty measures of oil' the Syriac, Arabic, and Septuagint all read 'twenty thousand,' as in Chronicles. Ellicott presses the same point: twenty measures of oil is so trifling beside twenty thousand of wheat — which itself agrees well with the ninety measures a day of the royal court in 1 Kings 4:22 — that it seems best to adopt the Greek reading of twenty thousand baths.

The one real debate here, then, is textual: whether the annual oil was 'twenty measures' (the received Hebrew) or 'twenty thousand baths' (the Septuagint, the versions, Chronicles, and Josephus). Clarke and Ellicott stand together for the larger figure on grounds of proportion; Gill works with the number before him. Beneath the dispute over digits the witnesses agree on the thing the verse records — a generous, standing recompense rendered 'year by year,' Israel's abundance paying honestly for Tyre's craft, so that the house of God was built by no man's loss. Gill's staggering total, grain to be numbered in the millions of pounds, and the versions' larger figure for the oil alike witness to a traffic conducted on a royal scale and sustained, as the verse says, 'year by year'; and whether the received Hebrew or the Greek reading be preferred, the interpreters agree that the arrangement was no single gift but a standing covenant of supply, Israel's harvest answering Tyre's craft for as long as the building should require.
12:
'And the Lord gave Solomon wisdom, as he promised him.' The witnesses read the verse as the seal on the whole transaction, tracing the peace between the kings back to its source in God's gift. The reformed Gill sees that wisdom displayed on every side of the story — in Solomon's preparations for the temple, in his shrewd agreements with Hiram for timber and workmen, and in his continuing and confirming the friendship with a king so serviceable to him. The Puritan Henry sets the gift above every earthly benefit: God gave Solomon wisdom, 'which was more and better than anything Hiram did or could give him'; and it was this that made Hiram love him and enabled Solomon to improve that kindness, so that the two were willing to 'ripen their mutual love into a mutual league,' that it might be lasting. From it Henry draws a maxim of friendship — it is wisdom to strengthen our bonds with those we find honest and fair, 'lest new friends prove not so firm and so kind as old.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch guard the reach of the statement. The remark that the Lord gave Solomon wisdom refers, they insist, not merely to the treaty he made with Hiram, through which he obtained materials and skilled workmen, but also to the wise use he made of the capacities of his own subjects for the work; and the verse thus stands at a hinge, closing the account of the dealings with Tyre and opening the section on the levy raised out of Israel.

The interpreters agree entirely. The clause 'as he promised him' points back to the gift bestowed at Gibeon, when God granted Solomon a wise and understanding heart (1 Kings 3:12); and here that promise is seen bearing visible fruit — in a temple wisely prepared, a treaty wisely struck, a friendship wisely kept, and a kingdom wisely marshalled. The peace and the league between Hiram and Solomon are, on every reading, not the achievement of policy alone but the outworking of a wisdom that was God's gift.
13:
The narrative turns from Tyre to Israel, and Solomon 'raised a levy out of all Israel' of thirty thousand men. The witnesses read the verse for its facts and for its shadow. The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work. Keil and Delitzsch identify the levy as tribute-labour (the word mas, as in 1 Kings 4:6) drawn from the whole nation, thirty thousand men sent up to Lebanon ten thousand a month in rotation; and they carefully distinguish these Israelite labourers from the remnants of the Canaanites who were made tribute-slaves (1 Kings 9:20; compare Joshua 16:10). Ellicott presses the harder note: this levy was exceptional, for 1 Kings 9:22 expressly says that 'of the children of Israel did Solomon make no bondmen.' Introduced first for the special service of God, it may, he fears, have been 'the beginning of what was hereafter an oppressive despotism'; and the whole description recalls to him the Egyptian bondage and the pyramids described by Herodotus — the vast sacrifice of human labour at which the great monuments of antiquity were reared.

The Wesleyan Clarke softens the picture with the detail of the rota: only ten thousand were employed at once, and those only for a month, after which they rested two months before returning; Adoniram was their superintendent, and all were Israelites. The Puritan Henry, with the reformed Gill, keeps the same balance: the Israelites had the lighter and more honourable work of felling and squaring timber, and Solomon 'takes care that they shall not be over-worked,' for 'great men ought to consider that their servants must rest as well as they.'

So the debate is really a question of harmony: how to reconcile this levy with the later word that Solomon made no Israelite bondmen. The witnesses answer together — the Israelites bore a light, rotated corvée and, under the chief officers, oversight of the far larger mass of foreign labourers (2 Chronicles 2:17), while the true bondage fell on the subject races. It is an honest note in the account: even the building of God's house was reared by real and costly human labour, and the seed of a later oppression lay hidden in the glory.
14:
The verse sets out the working of the levy and the officer who managed it, and the witnesses attend to the ordered, humane machinery of so vast an undertaking. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse the rotation exactly: ten thousand went up each month by courses, so that a man spent one month in Lebanon and two at home 'looking after the cultivation of their own ground,' with Adoniram set over the whole levy. They then read on into the greater numbers — the seventy thousand bearers of burdens and eighty thousand hewers on the mountains — and settle the sense of the verb hatsab: it is used almost always of quarrying and cutting stone (Deuteronomy 6:11; Isaiah 5:2; Proverbs 9:1; 2 Kings 12:13), and only once, in Isaiah 10:15, of cutting wood; so these hundred and fifty thousand are stone-workers, distinct from the thirty thousand who handled the timber.

The reformed Gill confirms that the ten thousand a month are the servants Solomon had promised to set alongside Hiram's men, assisting in felling the trees and squaring the timber in Lebanon. The Puritan Henry lingers, as at the previous verse, on the mercy of the arrangement: the two months' rest for each month's labour shows that even temple service was not to be turned into oppression, and that a master should remember his servants' need of rest.

The witnesses do not divide; they simply admire the order of it. A workforce numbered in the tens of thousands is marshalled into monthly courses, given seasons at home, and placed under a single responsible overseer, so that the labour is both immense and humane. And already the verse quietly draws the line the next verses will make plain — between the rotated Israelite levy, who came and went and returned to their fields, and the great enslaved multitude of bearers and hewers whose burden knew no such relief. Keil and Delitzsch's careful separation of the wood-labourers from the stone-workers, and Gill's note that the monthly courses laboured beside Hiram's Sidonians, together show a single enterprise wisely divided among many hands; while Henry's admiration for the rota is not sentiment but justice, since a servant pressed without rest is a servant wronged, and Solomon's ordering of the levy honours that even amid so great a demand. The verse thus prepares, by its very humaneness toward Israel, for the darker contrast of the servitude the next verse will record.
15:
The verse names the great mass of the labour force — seventy thousand that bore burdens and eighty thousand that hewed in the mountains — and the witnesses agree, against the lighter Israelite levy of the verses before, that these were not Israelites but strangers. The reformed Gill explains that the seventy thousand carried the stones from the quarries near Jerusalem to the city, and that they, with the hewers, 'were strangers in Israel.' The Wesleyan Clarke agrees flatly: 'these were all strangers, or proselytes, dwelling among the Israelites,' as the parallel record shows (2 Chronicles 2:17).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the distinction on textual grounds. The hundred and fifty thousand bearers of burdens and hewers of stone are not reckoned among the Israelites at all, being expressly separated from them; they were, rather, the remnant of the Canaanite peoples, the tribute-slaves who appear again as bondmen elsewhere in the reign. The Israelite thirty thousand handled the wood; this far greater body did the heavy quarrying and carrying of stone.

Here the Puritan Henry sounds the sober note the numbers invite. These, he says, 'were captives of other nations, who were to bear burdens and to hew stone'; and he marks the difference from the Israelite levy with a single grave observation — 'we read not that these had their resting times as the other had, for they were doomed to servitude.' The witnesses do not contend; together they hold the whole picture in view. The house of God rose by a labour that was partly a light and rotated service of Israel and partly the hard bondage of the stranger — a mingling of peoples that, in the mind of the interpreters, faintly prefigures the day when strangers and captives would be gathered, no longer as slaves but as sons, to build a greater temple. Gill's detail that the burden-bearers fetched the stones from quarries near Jerusalem, and Keil and Delitzsch's textual separation of these from the covenant people, together fix the identity of this great multitude as the subject races of the land; and Henry's grave contrast — Israel resting by courses, the stranger 'doomed to servitude' — is the honest shadow the chapter does not hide. Yet the interpreters do not leave it there, but read even this mingled and unequal labour as pointing beyond itself, toward the house not made with hands in which the once-far-off are built together for a habitation of God.
16:
Over the hundred and fifty thousand labourers stood a body of overseers, and the witnesses read the verse for the ordered hierarchy of the work and for the vexed question of its numbers. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse it closely: 'besides' (lebad, not counting) the princes, Solomon's officers set over the work, three thousand three hundred, who 'ruled over the people that wrought.' These cannot be a mere handful of head-inspectors, they argue, for the number is far too large and no lower tier is mentioned; the participle defines the noun — 'princes who were overseers.' They note, too, that at the close of all Solomon's building 1 Kings 9:23 names another five hundred and fifty such officers, and that the Chronicles give the totals differently, three thousand six hundred in 2 Chronicles 2:17 and two hundred and fifty in 2 Chronicles 8:10. Ellicott raises the further puzzle of who these officers were: one would have supposed them Israelites, as the five hundred and fifty of 1 Kings 9:23 clearly were, yet the figure in 2 Chronicles 2:18 seems to imply that they, like the overseers of Israel in the Egyptian bondage (Exodus 5:14), were drawn from the subject races.

The reformed Gill reads the office simply — the chief officers over the whole work, and especially over the hewing and carriage of the stone — and the Wesleyan Clarke notes only that the parallel in 2 Chronicles 2:18 gives three thousand six hundred, the same number the Septuagint has here.

The debate, then, is one of harmony and of history: the differing totals across Kings and Chronicles (three thousand three hundred, three thousand six hundred, five hundred and fifty, two hundred and fifty), and whether the overseers were Israelite or foreign. The witnesses resolve it by distinguishing classes and tiers of officer, and by allowing for variation between the received texts. Beneath the numbers they agree on the plain sense — that so enormous a work required a whole army of supervisors as truly as of labourers, and that God's house was reared not by confusion but by careful, graded order.
17:
The foundation is laid, and the witnesses gather on the 'great, costly, hewed stones,' reading them for their scale, their cost, and their hidden glory. The grammatical-historical commentators secure the sense of the words. Keil and Delitzsch insist that yekaroth means not 'heavy' (a superfluous remark of large stones) but 'costly, valuable,' stones that cost much money; and they extend 'the foundation' beyond the temple-house to the vast substructions of the whole temple area, the mighty walls Josephus describes, which may have been the work of centuries, the squared gazith stones running, by 1 Kings 7:10, to ten and eight cubits. Ellicott agrees that such stones were needed not for the small temple itself but for the substructure that squared off the irregular summit of Mount Moriah; vast stones, he notes, are still to be seen there and referred by many to Solomon's age, and 'the labour of transport must have been enormous, especially as all were worked beforehand' (1 Kings 6:7). The Wesleyan Clarke distinguishes the three terms simply — great in dimension, costly in the labour and time of cutting, hewed in being squared and polished.

The reformed Gill reads 'great' of quantity as well as size — large stones fit to lay in the foundation, strong and durable against all the injuries of time, as Josephus also reports.

The Puritan Henry lifts the founding into a lesson. Solomon was himself present at the work, and the first stone was laid with solemnity; he 'commanded, and they brought costly stones for the foundation,' doing everything like himself, generously, so that costly stones were laid — or rather 'buried' — out of sight where worse might have served. For Christ, laid for a foundation, is 'an elect and precious stone' (Isaiah 28:16), and the foundations of the church are laid with sapphires (Isaiah 54:11; compare Revelation 21:19). From this Henry draws the point on which the witnesses land together: the sincerity that is our gospel perfection 'obliges us to lay our foundation firm, and to bestow most pains on that part of our religion which lies out of the sight of men.'
18:
The account of the preparations closes with the builders at work, and the witnesses fasten on a proper name that an old translation had turned into a trade — the 'stone-squarers,' who are really the Giblites. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the verse so that Solomon's builders and Hiram's builders, 'even the Giblites,' hewed and prepared the wood and the stones for the house; the phrase 'the Giblites' defines Hiram's men more precisely. They are the inhabitants of Gebal, called Byblos by the Greeks, north of Beirut (Joshua 13:5), the nearest of the larger Phoenician towns to the cedar forest; and since Ezekiel 27:9 shows the Giblites experienced in shipbuilding, they were probably skilful builders generally, the fittest of Hiram's subjects to prepare the timber and stone. Ellicott confirms that 'stone-squarers' is 'a curious gloss on the proper name Giblites,' the men of Gebal (named in Ezekiel 27:9 and probably Psalms 83:7); and since they are distinguished from Hiram's builders, he suggests they may have been serfs under them, as the Canaanites were under Solomon's. The Wesleyan Clarke reaches the same conclusion, that the margin's 'Giblites' is to be preferred, though the ancient versions mostly adopted the reading of stone-squarers because the context shows the men clever at cutting stone.

The reformed Gill draws the plain inference that Solomon had from Hiram hewers of stone as well as of wood, so that timber and stone alike were now made ready.

With this the witnesses close the chapter in agreement. There is no real dispute here, only the recovery of a name the translators had lost; and the finished picture is a striking one. The house of the God of Israel was made ready by Israelite levy, by Tyrian craft, and by Giblite skill, before ever a wall was raised in 1 Kings 6. It is the very mingling Henry heard foretold in the prophet's vision of the sons of strangers building the walls of the gospel temple (Isaiah 60:10) — a foretaste, the interpreters together sense, of the Gentiles gathered in to build, with Israel, the one house of God.
